text:
Even those familiar with the Gospel of John are likely to think of the account of Jesus’ journey to Judea in John 3:22–36 with little more than passing attention. Even less attention has been given to the geographical context of this passage, due in part to a lack of inquiry into the identity of the Jew (Ἰουδαῖος) who disagrees with Jesus’ disciples and the disciples of John the Baptist in John 3:25. This article attempts to address some of these concerns by offering an examination of the geography of Jesus’ ministry from John 2:13 to 4:54.

In the Fourth Gospel, the mission of Jesus is structured along geographical lines following a pattern similar to that described in Acts 1:8—that is, from Jerusalem via Judea and Samaria (though without continuing on as does the gospel of Luke in Acts 8:14, 9:31 with Philip and Peter in Samaria and then Peter in Judea). Apparently the structure of the mission as seen in the geographical movement of Jesus serves to place the conversion of Israel as a whole between the before-change and the after-change scenes before the religious and theological conflicts between Jesus and the Jewish authorities in chs 5-10. Because the Judean mission is included in this total mission to Israel prior to the confrontations in chs 5-10, the term Ἰουδαῖος in John 3:25 does not refer to Judaism or to any ethnic or religious distinctions between Judaeans and Galileans that would be relevant to Jesus’ comments about himself in ch 7. Rather the term Judean describes a particular geographical area, and "Judean" is what John 3:25 intends in this context.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
